null_hypothesis: There is no linear relationship between daily_return and volume.
alternative_hypothesis: There is a linear relationship between daily_return and volume.
interpretation: The relationship is weak, positive, and statistically significant at the 5% level.

null_hypothesis: There is no linear relationship between daily_return and turnover_cr.
alternative_hypothesis: There is a linear relationship between daily_return and turnover_cr.
interpretation: The relationship is weak, positive, and statistically significant at the 5% level.

null_hypothesis: There is no linear relationship between daily_return and deliverable_percent.
alternative_hypothesis: There is a linear relationship between daily_return and deliverable_percent.
interpretation: The relationship is weak, negative, and statistically significant at the 5% level.

null_hypothesis: There is no linear relationship between volatility_percent and annualized_return_percent.
alternative_hypothesis: There is a linear relationship between volatility_percent and annualized_return_percent.
interpretation: The relationship is weak, negative, and not statistically significant at the 5% level.

null_hypothesis: There is no linear relationship between liquidity_score and risk_adjusted_return.
alternative_hypothesis: There is a linear relationship between liquidity_score and risk_adjusted_return.
interpretation: The relationship is weak, negative, and not statistically significant at the 5% level.

null_hypothesis: High-delivery and low-delivery stocks have the same average daily return.
alternative_hypothesis: High-delivery and low-delivery stocks have different average daily returns.
interpretation: Independent two-sample t-test does not reject the null hypothesis at the 5% level. There is not enough evidence to say delivery intensity changes average stock-level returns.

null_hypothesis: Sector return distributions are the same across industries.
alternative_hypothesis: At least one sector has a different return distribution.
interpretation: Kruskal-Wallis test rejects the null hypothesis at the 5% level. Sector return dispersion is statistically detectable, but the test reflects distribution differences as much as mean differences.
